did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning methods, specifically style transfer (ST) and deep blending (DB), to generate realistic images of darker skin colors for dermatology data augmentation. Additionally, a convolutional neural network (CNN) was trained on these generated images to assess their effect on skin lesion classification accuracy (malignant vs. benign).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'Original Paper' and presents original experimental work. It details a study with specific Background, Objective, Methods (collecting images from DermNet NZ, ISIC, and Dermatology Atlas; applying style transfer and deep blending; training a CNN), Results (quantitative and qualitative metrics like BRISQUE, SSIM, accuracy, AUC), and Conclusions. It reports new results from data augmentation and model training rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Improving Skin Color Diversity in Cancer Detection: Deep Learning Approach
brief_summary: This study addresses the underrepresentation of dark skin in dermatology resources by developing a deep learning approach to generate realistic images of darker skin tones for data augmentation. The researchers collected 1701 clinical images of malignant and benign lesions from DermNet NZ, ISIC-2018, and Dermatology Atlas, then applied style transfer (ST) and deep blending (DB) methods to create darker skin variants. Image quality was evaluated quantitatively and qualitatively, with ST outperforming DB in realism and disease presentation similarity scores. A convolutional neural network was trained on the generated images to assess their impact on skin lesion classification accuracy. The model achieved a classification accuracy of 0.76 and an AUC of 0.72 when using the generated images, compared to 0.56 accuracy and 0.63 AUC without them. The authors conclude that deep learning-generated diverse skin images can improve the generalizability of AI skin cancer diagnosis applications.

lesion_types_studied.lesion_type: Malignant and benign skin lesions (binary classification target)
lesion_types_studied.details: The primary classification task was binary: categorizing images as malignant or benign. The study collected 1701 clinical images representing several malignant and benign skin conditions from DermNet NZ, ISIC-2018, and Dermatology Atlas.

all_task_types.details: Binary classification task where a convolutional neural network (CNN) was trained to categorize images as malignant or benign. The model achieved a classification accuracy of 0.76 and an AUC of 0.72 when using generated images for data augmentation.

datasets_used.description: A publicly available skin image repository containing clinical images of skin conditions.

use.validation: Part of Set A (1094 images combined with ISIC) used for validating the classifier.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate the uncertainty of reported metrics including loss of realism scores, disease presentation similarity scores, BRISQUE scores, SSIM scores, classification accuracy, and AUC.
To estimate the uncertainty of the reported results, specifically for proportions such as the loss of realism score and disease presentation similarity score.
To investigate the significance of predictors (background, years of experience, real score) on the outcome (generated score) and to model the relationship between generated and real scores.
To compare the areas under the curve (AUC) of the model with and without generated images.

hypothesis_testing.test: Linear regression (for predictor significance) and DeLong test (for AUC comparison)
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: For linear regression: The predictors (background, years of experience, real score) have no significant effect on the outcome (generated score). For DeLong test: There is no difference in the area under the curve (AUC) between the model with generated images and the model without.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: For linear regression: At least one predictor has a significant effect on the outcome. For DeLong test: There is a significant difference in the AUC between the two models.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Linear regression results indicated that background and years of experience were not significant predictors (P=.96, P=.65, P=.61, P=.45, P=.65), while the real score was a significant predictor (P<.001). The DeLong test was used to compare AUCs, with the model using generated images achieving a significantly higher AUC (0.72) compared to the model without (0.63), as indicated by the non-overlapping 95% confidence intervals (0.67-0.77 vs 0.58-0.68) and the context of improvement.

Image Quality Evaluation (Style Transfer.notes: ST achieved higher average SSIM scores in all skin tones compared to DB.

Qualitative Assessment (Dermatologist Di.notes: Dermatologists correctly diagnosed lesions in the generated images.

Classification Model Performance (Set B.notes: Model 2 (ST augmentation) showed the best performance. The difference in AUC between Model 2 and Model 1 was significant (P<.001), and between Model 2 and Model 3 was significant (P=.03). There was no significant difference in AUC between Model 2 and Model 4 (P=.35).

Recall and Misdiagnosis Rates (Generated.notes: Basal cell carcinoma had the lowest average recall in generated images compared to real recall. Melanoma was best identified in dark skin color.

architecture_details.customization: Customized by adding an average pooling layer, a fully connected layer, and a SoftMax layer for binary classification (malignant vs. benign). The first 4 blocks of the ResNet-50 were frozen during transfer learning.
architecture_details.layers: 5 stages (blocks) in the ResNet-50 architecture; last block trained with newly added layers.

training_details.learning_rate_schedule: Incrementally reduced when there was no improvement in validation accuracy for 5 consecutive epochs
training_details.hardware: Google Collaboratory Pro with NVIDIA Tesla P100 GPU
data_augmentation: Generated tan, brown, and black images via Style Transfer (Model 2)
training_details.dataset_split: Set A (1094 images) split into 80% training (875 images) and 20% validation (219 images). Set B (607 images) used for testing.

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer followed by SoftMax

preprocessing_techniques: Style Transfer (ST) to generate images with darker skin colors from lighter skin images
Deep Blending (DB) to generate images with darker skin colors
Target skin color selection using Individual Typology Angle (ITA) calculated from input transformed images
Mapping ITA to predefined skin classes (tan, brown, black)
Data augmentation via geometric transformations (flipping, rotating, adding noise)
Data augmentation via combining generated images with transformed images
Freezing the first 4 blocks of the ResNet-50 architecture during transfer learning

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Generated realistic images of darker skin colors (tan, brown, and black) from lighter skin content images using a VGG-19 based deep learning method. Used to augment the original training dataset (Set A) to improve skin color diversity for the CNN classifier.

Generated images with darker skin colors by blending the border of the disease region from the content image with the style image. Evaluated as an alternative augmentation method but found to be inferior to Style Transfer in realism and disease presentation similarity.
Applied to the original images for augmentation, including flipping, rotating, and adding noise. Used in Model 3 and combined with generated images in Model 4.

Augmented the training images with both the Style Transfer-generated images (tan, brown, black) and the geometrically transformed images (flipping, rotating, noise). This approach was used in Model 4.

conclusions: Deep learning approaches, specifically style transfer (ST), can generate realistic skin lesion images that improve the skin color diversity of dermatology atlases.

The style transfer method outperformed deep blending in generating realistic images, achieving a lower loss of realism score (0.23 vs 0.63) and higher disease presentation similarity score (0.44 vs 0.17).

The generated images exhibited high realism, with 62.2% of votes in a visual Turing test classifying them as real, and dermatologists correctly diagnosed lesions in the generated images with an average rate of 0.75.

Training a convolutional neural network with generated images improved skin lesion classification accuracy (0.76 vs 0.56) and area under the curve (0.72 vs 0.63) compared to a model without the generated images.

Geometric transformations alone did not significantly increase model performance compared to no augmentation, while combining generated images with geometric transformations showed no significant difference in AUC compared to using generated images alone.
The diversified image bank demonstrates the potential of developing generalizable artificial intelligence skin cancer diagnosis applications.
Limitations acknowledged include that lesion pigmentation is not the only factor characterizing skin cancer in people of color, and other disease morphological features need to be integrated.
Future work is needed to improve classification accuracy using different CNN architectures and ensemble learning, and to incorporate text features representing skin cancer clinical presentation.
Images with real pathology in people of color are required to improve model training and validation.
important_remarks: The study relies on generating synthetic images of darker skin tones from lighter skin images using style transfer and deep blending, rather than using real clinical images of people of color, which limits the direct clinical validation of the generated data's diagnostic utility.

The classification model was trained on Set A (1094 images from DermNet NZ and ISIC-2018) and tested on Set B (607 images from Dermatology Atlas); while Set B had a more diverse skin tone distribution, it is a single external dataset, limiting the robustness of the generalizability claims.

The paper acknowledges that lesion pigmentation is not the only factor characterizing skin cancer in people of color, and other morphological features were not integrated into the model, representing a significant clinical limitation.

The study does not report performance metrics stratified by specific skin tones for the final classification model, making it difficult to assess if the improvement in accuracy (0.76 vs 0.56) applies equally across all skin types.

The qualitative assessment of image realism involved a Visual Turing Test with 54 participants, but the paper notes that human judgment might vary based on skin color, introducing potential bias in the realism evaluation.

The study uses a binary classification task (malignant vs. benign) rather than specific cancer types, which may mask performance disparities for specific high-risk lesions like melanoma in darker skin tones.

The authors explicitly state that images with real pathology in people of color are required to improve model training and validation, highlighting the current insufficiency of the generated data for final clinical deployment.
